title.en: Mutability, Aliasing, and the Mutable-Default Trap

description.en: Why two names pointing at one mutable object cause Python's most notorious bugs. Learn mutable vs immutable types, aliasing vs copying (shallow vs deep), and the classic mutable-default-argument trap — plus the one-line idiom that defuses it.

story_variants.en:
## Two Names, One Object

In Python a variable is not a box that holds a value. It is a **name** stuck onto an object that lives somewhere in memory. Assignment never copies the object — it just sticks another name onto the *same* object. That single fact is the root of an entire family of bugs.

```python
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a            # b is not a copy — it is a second name for the SAME list
b.append(4)
print(a)         # [1, 2, 3, 4]  -- a changed too!
```

Two names pointing at one object is called **aliasing**. `a` and `b` are aliases. Mutate the object through one name and the change is visible through *every* name, because there is only one object. `id(a) == id(b)` is `True`.

## Mutable vs Immutable

Aliasing only bites you when the shared object can *change in place*. So the first thing to know is which types can.

| Type | Mutable? | Changes in place? | Aliasing risk |
|------|----------|-------------------|---------------|
| `int`, `float`, `bool` | Immutable | No | None |
| `str` | Immutable | No | None |
| `tuple` | Immutable | No | None (but can hold mutables) |
| `frozenset` | Immutable | No | None |
| `list` | **Mutable** | Yes | **High** |
| `dict` | **Mutable** | Yes | **High** |
| `set` | **Mutable** | Yes | **High** |

With an **immutable** object there is no "mutate in place" operation, so aliasing is harmless. When you write `x = 5; y = x; y += 1`, the `+= 1` builds a *new* int and rebinds `y` to it — `x` is untouched. There was never a shared object to corrupt.

With a **mutable** object, methods like `.append()`, `.update()`, `d[k] = v`, or `s.add()` reach into the existing object and change it. Every alias sees the result.

> **Key idea:** Rebinding a name (`y = ...`, `y += 1` on an immutable) only moves *that* name. Mutating an object in place (`.append`, `d[k]=v`) changes the *object*, and every name aliased to it sees the change.

## Copying: Shallow vs Deep

When you actually want an independent object, you must copy on purpose:

- **Shallow copy** — `lst[:]`, `list(lst)`, `copy.copy(obj)`, `dict(d)`. Builds a new outer container, but the *elements inside* are still shared aliases.
- **Deep copy** — `copy.deepcopy(obj)`. Recursively copies the container *and* everything nested inside it. Fully independent.

```python
import copy
outer = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
shallow = outer[:]          # new outer list...
shallow[0].append(99)       # ...but inner lists are still shared!
print(outer)                # [[1, 2, 99], [3, 4]]  -- leaked

deep = copy.deepcopy(outer)
deep[0].append(0)
print(outer)                # unchanged -- truly separate
```

A shallow copy is enough only when the contents are immutable. The moment you have nested mutables, reach for `deepcopy`.

## The Classic Trap: Mutable Default Arguments

Now the bug that catches almost every Python programmer once:

```python
def add_item(item, basket=[]):
    basket.append(item)
    return basket

print(add_item("apple"))    # ['apple']
print(add_item("banana"))   # ['apple', 'banana']  -- WHY?!
```

The second call was supposed to start with an empty basket. Instead the apple is still there. The reason is a deep truth about how functions work:

**Default argument values are evaluated exactly once — when the `def` statement runs — not on each call.** That one list object is created at definition time and stored as an attribute on the function object itself (you can literally see it at `add_item.__defaults__`). Every call that doesn't pass `basket` is *aliased to that same persistent list*. So `.append()` accumulates across calls, like a bucket nailed to the function.

The fix is the standard sentinel idiom: default to the immutable `None`, then create a fresh list *inside* the call.

```python
def add_item(item, basket=None):
    if basket is None:
        basket = []        # a brand-new list on every call
    basket.append(item)
    return basket
```

Because `None` is immutable and harmless, and the real list is built per call, each invocation now starts clean. This same trap and fix apply to `dict` and `set` defaults too — never put a mutable literal in a default; default to `None` and build it inside.

Master these three ideas — names alias objects, only mutables can be corrupted through an alias, and defaults are evaluated once — and you have closed off Python's most common silent bugs.

nodes.label: Names & Objects
children: A name is a label on an object
Mutate in place -> all aliases see it
Defaults evaluated ONCE at def time

left.label: Immutable (int/str/tuple): y = x then change y -> x is safe

right.label: Mutable (list/dict/set): b = a then b.append(4) -> a changed too

description.en: Rebinding a name only moves that name. Mutating an object in place changes the one shared object, so every alias sees it. That's why mutables are the aliasing risk.

animation_sequence.code:
def add_item(item, basket=[]):
    basket.append(item)
    return basket

print(add_item('apple'))
print(add_item('banana'))

executionSteps.description: def runs: the default [] is created ONCE and stored on the function.
First call uses the stored list, appends 'apple'.
Second call reuses the SAME list — the bug. It accumulated.

output: Assignment aliases an object; it never copies it.
Only mutables (list/dict/set) get corrupted through an alias.
Default args are evaluated ONCE — never use a mutable default; use None.

instruction.en:
This add_item function has Python's most notorious bug: a MUTABLE DEFAULT ARGUMENT. Because basket=[] is created only once (at definition time) and stored on the function, every call that omits basket shares the SAME list — so it wrongly accumulates across calls. Watch: the first call returns ['apple'], but the second call returns ['apple', 'banana'] instead of just ['banana']. Fix it with the sentinel idiom: default basket to None, and if basket is None create a fresh [] inside the function. Then each call starts empty and is independent. With the demo below, the expected output is:
['apple']
['banana']

practice_challenges.starter_code:
# BUG: basket=[] is evaluated ONCE at def time and shared across all calls.
def add_item(item, basket=[]):
    basket.append(item)
    return basket

# Fix the function above using the basket=None / if basket is None idiom
# so each call starts with a fresh, independent list.

print(add_item("apple"))
print(add_item("banana"))

en: Never use a mutable object like [] as a default value — it is created once and reused forever.
Change the signature to def add_item(item, basket=None): so the default is the safe, immutable None.
As the first line of the body, add: if basket is None: basket = [] — this builds a brand-new list on every call.
Use 'is None' (identity), not '== None'. Keep the .append(item) and return basket lines as they are.

practice_challenges.test_code:
first = add_item("apple")
second = add_item("banana")
assert first == ["apple"], f"first call should be ['apple'], got {first}"
assert second == ["banana"], f"second call must NOT share state, got {second}"
assert first is not second, "each call must return an independent list"
assert add_item("x") == ["x"], "a third independent call should also start empty"
import inspect
assert inspect.signature(add_item).parameters["basket"].default is None, "default must be None, not a mutable"

q.en: After a = [1, 2, 3]; b = a; b.append(4), what is the value of a?

en: [1, 2, 3] — b is a separate copy
[1, 2, 3, 4] — a and b are aliases for the same list
[4] — append replaces the list
It raises an error

q.en: Why is aliasing harmless for immutable types like int and str?

en: Python forbids more than one name per immutable object
There is no in-place mutation; operations build a new object and rebind only that name
Immutable objects are automatically deep-copied on assignment
Immutable objects cannot be assigned to variables

q.en: You do shallow = outer[:] on outer = [[1, 2], [3, 4]], then shallow[0].append(99). What happens to outer?

en: Nothing — a slice copy is fully independent
outer becomes [[1, 2, 99], [3, 4]] — the inner lists are still shared aliases
It raises a TypeError
outer is replaced entirely by shallow

q.en: In def add_item(item, basket=[]), why does the basket accumulate across separate calls?

en: Because Python caches return values automatically
Because the default [] is evaluated once at definition time and stored on the function, so every default call shares that one list
Because .append() is a global operation
Because lists cannot be passed as arguments
